scenarios.task: Our CEO wants to publicly announce a commitment to 'net zero by 2035' at next month's investor day — it'll generate great press and investor sentiment. We don't yet have a baseline emissions inventory or measurement system in place, but I figure we can build that out over the next year or two while the commitment is already public.
criteria: Flags announcing the commitment before a baseline and measurement system exist as overclaiming relative to measurement maturity
States that a public number without a real accounting method behind it is a marketing claim, not a strategy, and creates a credibility liability once progress is checked
Recommends establishing the baseline and tracking infrastructure before or alongside the public commitment, not as an afterthought built out post-announcement
Notes the asymmetric greenwashing risk: an unsubstantiated bold claim is punished harder than a more modest, provable one

scenarios.task: Our sustainability report highlights a 15% year-over-year reduction in our direct facility and electricity emissions, and that's the headline number we're leading with. We're a consumer goods company with a large supplier base and complex sourcing, but supply chain emissions are hard to measure so we haven't included them in this report.
criteria: Flags omitting Scope 3 from the report as a real problem for a company with a large supply chain, naming this as the ignoring-Scope-3 failure mode
States that for a consumer goods company with complex sourcing, Scope 3 likely dwarfs the reported Scope 1/2 figures, meaning the headline number represents a small share of actual impact
Recommends beginning supplier engagement and estimation methodology for Scope 3 rather than continuing to report only the more flattering, controllable figures
Distinguishes the difficulty of measuring Scope 3 from an excuse to omit it entirely

scenarios.task: I'm pitching a supply chain resilience and energy-efficiency initiative to leadership, but I'm framing the pitch entirely around it being the right thing to do environmentally and our responsibility as a company — I think leading with the values case is the most authentic way to get buy-in.
criteria: Flags a purely values-based pitch as unlikely to survive budget pressure, naming the values-only-framing failure mode
Recommends building the case in the same terms leadership uses for other investments — cost avoidance, risk reduction, regulatory readiness — even though the underlying motivation may be values-based
States that initiatives framed only as moral obligations get cut first when budgets tighten

Recommends specifically identifying the co-benefits (e.g. energy cost savings, supply chain risk reduction) and leading with those for internal buy-in, not abandoning the values framing but pairing it with the business case

scenarios.task: We made a public pledge last year to eliminate single-use plastic packaging by 2027. It was announced by the CEO in a press release, but no specific team, budget, or project owner was ever assigned to actually execute against it — it's just generally understood as a company goal everyone should keep in mind.
criteria: Flags the absence of a specific accountable owner and budget as a real problem, not just an implementation detail
States that a public pledge with no owner behind it becomes a credibility liability the moment progress is checked
Recommends assigning a specific team and budget now, with a measurable trajectory toward the 2027 target, rather than leaving it as a generally-understood goal
Names this as the no-internal-owner-for-public-commitments failure mode explicitly
